text: The lives of most people who have ever lived will never be remembered or mentioned in any way. Fortunately for those of us who find ourselves as followers of Abraham, God has given us a remarkable legacy of the man of God through the biblical narrative of Abraham. The most well-known part of this narrative will be the account of Abraham's intercession for Sodom and Gomorrah in Genesis 18:16–33. In this passage God determines to destroy the city because of the wickedness of its people. Abraham, however, acts as an intercessor in a significant way, and as a result Lot and his two daughters are saved. This thesis will investigate Abraham's role as an intercessor through a Pentecostal theology of the narrative of his life and the theological significance of Abraham's intercession. It makes use of narrative interpretation methods, incorporating a full review of the literature associated with Genesis and the life and legacy of Abraham. In doing so, this research reveals that Abraham's intercession is a prime exemplar of the type of faith that is valued in the Pentecostal tradition, which also understands the act of intercession to be an expression of compassion. The intercession of Abraham provides a comprehensive model of what God may be calling the contemporary church to be involved in as a mission in the Spirit.
